title: UHTTP Headers for Character Set Encoding
breadcrumbs: UHTTP Headers for Character Set Encoding
html:
UHTTP Headers for Character Set Encoding

Add to My Topics

  • Last UpdatedJan 26, 2023
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

By default, the UHTTP component converts the content of an HTTP request to the character set specified by $SYS_CHARSET. However, it is possible to specify another character set, such as Unicode.

The UHTTP component can support Unicode, and any of the other character sets Uniface supports, such as Windows-1252 (Western), ISO-8859-1 (Western), or Shift_JIS (Japanese).

To enable Unicode, you must specify the Unicode character set in the HTML Content-Type header, or for XML, in the XML declaration. For example

  • In the header: Content-Type= application/soap+xml; charset=UTF-8

  • In the XML declaration: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" ?>

This ensures that the data is sent as UTF-8, and also sets the default for the expected response to UTF-8.

If a different character set is required for the response, you can add a Accept-Charset header to inform the server which character sets the client can accept, as follows:

"Accept-Charset: CharacterSet{;q=N}{, CharacterSet{;q=Value}}"

  • CharacterSet is an internationally recognized character set name, such as utf-8, Windows-1252, ISO-8859-1, or Shift_JIS (Japanese).

  • q=Value is the quality factor, which defines a preference for the character set to be returned. Values can be from 0 to 1, with 1 being the highest preference, and 0 indicating the client does not accept it. If omitted, it defaults to 1.

The Accept-Charset header directs the server to send data in one of the specified character sets. For servers that do not honor the request for a specific characters set, the Accept-Charset header specifies a default character set, which is used if the server also does not supply a character set in its header.

According to the HTTP specification, ISO-8859-1;q=1 is always implicitly added, so if you have defined a different accept character set, you may still get ISO-8859-1. To prevent this, it is best to explicitly add ISO-8859-1 and set the quality number to a lower preference, such as q=0 or q=.3. For example:

;vHeaders contains an associative list of HTTP headers

putitem/id/case vHeaders, "Content-Type", "application/xhtml+xml; charset=UTF-8"
putitem/id/case vHeaders, "Accept-Charset", "ISO-8859-1 ; q = 0.4, UTF-16BE; q=0.9"

The UHTTP component takes the character set with the highest quality number as its default. If multiple character sets have the same quality number assigned, the first one defined is used, and ISO-8859-1 is last.

If the Content-Type header does not specify the character set encoding, and the MIME type ends with xml (for example, .../xml or .../xhtml+xml), the character set encoding specified in the <?xml... ?> header is used. If it is not defined, it defaults to UTF-8. Other MIME types default to $SYS_CHARSET.

The header Content-Length should not be set before calling UHTTP. UHTTP will calculate this after conversion to the appropriate character set.
